target.title: Memory/QMD: isolate mcporter sidecars per agent
target.body:
## Summary

Isolates QMD mcporter configuration per agent while preserving user/workspace-owned mcporter definitions that must stay external. For agents without an explicit user-owned mcporter server definition, OpenClaw generates a per-agent stdio config under `$STATE/agents/<agentId>/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json` with manager-scoped QMD env vars.

Supersedes #79745. This replacement PR keeps the QMD/mcporter fix and drops the unrelated Skill Workshop i18n commit that accidentally widened the superseding branch.

## Linked context

Related #79745.

## Behavior addressed

- Remote QMD servers with auth material (`headers`, env, URL credentials, refresh metadata, token-like query params) stay external and use the original mcporter config.
- Servers with `lifecycle`, daemon logging, relative `cwd`, or relative `path` are treated as user-owned and kept external.
- Bare stdio QMD servers are rewritten into a generated per-agent config under OpenClaw state.
- `startDaemon=false` omits the keep-alive lifecycle in the generated config so mcporter does not auto-launch the daemon.
- Snake-case remote endpoint keys (`base_url`, `server_url`) are recognized alongside camelCase.
- Project-scoped mcporter configs are probed before legacy home fallback.
- Discovery mode uses a clean `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` so OpenClaw state dirs do not shadow user/project configs.
- External-mode calls set `MCPORTER_CONFIG` to the discovered source path so the original config is reused.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: QMD memory search through mcporter must keep each agent on its own mcporter config/daemon state while preserving configured user/workspace mcporter servers that contain auth, lifecycle, logging, cwd/path, or other mcporter-owned behavior.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node v24.15.0, mcporter 0.10.2, qmd 2.1.0 (bab86d5), OpenClaw built from `fix/qmd-mcporter-agent-isolation`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
pnpm build
export OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_DIR="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/config"
export XDG_CONFIG_HOME="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/xdg-config"
export XDG_CACHE_HOME="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/xdg-cache"
export QMD_CONFIG_DIR="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/qmd-config"
unset MCPORTER_CONFIG

./openclaw.mjs agents add proof-a --non-interactive --workspace "$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/workspace-a"
./openclaw.mjs agents add proof-b --non-interactive --workspace "$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/workspace-b"
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.backend qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.command qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.enabled true
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.serverName qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.startDaemon false

./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "test" --agent proof-a
./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "test" --agent proof-b
```

- Evidence after fix:

Per-agent generated configs are isolated:

```text
$STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  command: qmd
  args: ["mcp"]
  XDG_CONFIG_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-config
  QMD_CONFIG_DIR: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-config/qmd
  XDG_CACHE_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-cache
  lifecycle: absent when startDaemon=false

$STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  command: qmd
  args: ["mcp"]
  XDG_CONFIG_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-config
  QMD_CONFIG_DIR: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-config/qmd
  XDG_CACHE_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-cache
  lifecycle: absent when startDaemon=false
```

`startDaemon=true` adds the keep-alive lifecycle:

```text
$STATE/agents/proof-daemon/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  lifecycle: { mode: "keep-alive", idleTimeoutMs: 300000 }
```

Remote auth config stays external:

```text
User ~/.mcporter/mcporter.json contains:
  base_url: https://qmd.example.com/mcp
  headers.Authorization: Bearer redacted-token

./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "external" --agent proof-external
  mcporter call qmd.query ... failed with ENOTFOUND qmd.example.com

No $STATE/agents/proof-external/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json was generated; auth material was not copied into OpenClaw state.
```

Project config wins over home config:

```text
$WORKSPACE/config/mcporter.json contains qmd cwd ./project-cwd and lifecycle keep-alive.
./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "project" --agent proof-project
  mcporter retries the configured qmd server and preserves the relative cwd from project config.
```

- Observed result after fix: bare stdio QMD servers are isolated per agent, daemon startup follows `startDaemon`, and user-owned remote/project/lifecycle/logging/cwd/path mcporter definitions remain external instead of being copied into generated OpenClaw-managed state.
- What was not tested: Windows paths. Broken explicit `MCPORTER_CONFIG` still silently falls back to generated mode for the default `qmd` server; this is pre-existing behavior.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the remote-auth proof used a redacted/unresolvable test endpoint to prove OpenClaw reused the external mcporter config without copying auth-bearing state.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): previous reviews on #79745 confirmed current main passed QMD-scoped XDG env into mcporter, which could redirect mcporter discovery/state into per-agent QMD paths.

## Tests and validation

Fresh validation after cleaning this superseding PR branch on head `e1ec585278`:

```sh
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts
```

Observed local result:

```text
qmd-manager.test.ts: 157 tests passed
agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts: 33 tests passed
git diff --check: clean
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`, in the safer direction: auth-bearing mcporter definitions stay external and are not copied into OpenClaw-managed generated state.

Highest-risk area: mcporter config discovery and upgrade compatibility for existing QMD memory users.

Mitigation: preserve explicit/project/home mcporter config sources when the server definition contains user-owned behavior, generate per-agent configs only for bare stdio QMD servers, and cover the classifier/generation matrix in `qmd-manager.test.ts`.

## Current review state

Next action: ClawSweeper re-review on the cleaned head.

Still waiting on: maintainer/ClawSweeper review after the branch cleanup.

Addressed bot/reviewer comments:

- Supersedes accidentally closed #79745.
- Kept the QMD/mcporter fixes from #79745.
- Dropped unrelated Skill Workshop i18n commit from this superseding PR.
- Rebased onto current `upstream/main`.

target.title: fix(sdk): narrow SpawnOptions cwd type for tsgo compatibility
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`check-test-types` CI lane fails on all recent PRs because `packages/sdk/src/package.e2e.test.ts:127` passes `spec.options.cwd` (typed as `string | URL` from `SpawnOptions`) to `runCommand` which expects `cwd: string`.

Error: `error TS2322: Type 'string | URL' is not assignable to type 'string'.`

## Why This Change Was Made

The one-line fix adds a type assertion `(spec.options.cwd as string)` to narrow the type before the `??` fallback. This is safe because `spec.options.cwd` is set from `options.cwd` which is already `string` in the only call site (`runPnpmCommand`).

## User Impact

Fixes CI for all open PRs. No runtime behavior change.

## Evidence

- Before: `pnpm tsgo:test:packages` exits with code 2
- After: `pnpm tsgo:test:packages` passes cleanly

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): surface accountId on agent hook context
target.body:
## TL;DR

Adds an optional `accountId` to the plugin **agent hook context** so account-aware plugins can read which channel account a turn belongs to, instead of reverse-engineering it from `sessionKey` (which doesn't carry it for group or cron runs). Fully additive.

**Maintainer decision requested:** `accountId` becomes part of the public plugin SDK hook context. The name mirrors the `accountId` already on the hook *event* types (e.g. `before_agent_run`) — this just brings the *context* in line. OK to lock in `accountId` as the canonical field?

## Why

Multi-account deployments (several Feishu apps / Slack workspaces on one instance) already track an `accountId` internally for routing and auth, but plugins receiving agent hooks have no reliable way to recover it:

- `sessionKey` only encodes the account on some surfaces — **absent** in group conversations (`agent:<id>:<provider>:group:<chatId>`) and cron runs (`agent:main:cron:<job>:run:<id>`).
- Indexing a store by `sessionKey` races when multiple bots are @-ed in one group at the same time.

Surfacing the turn-bound `accountId` at the runtime seam lets a plugin stay **stateless** across DM / group-multi-bot / cron / subagent with a single hook — no sessionKey parsing, no shared-store workaround.

## What changed

- **`PluginHookAgentContext`** — new optional `accountId?: string` (public SDK surface).
- **`buildAgentHookContextChannelFields`** — surfaces `accountId`, following the exact pattern the shared builder already uses for `channel` / `chatId` / `senderId`. Accepts the existing internal `agentAccountId` as an alias so all current spread call sites forward it for free (no rename churn); `accountId` wins. Alias is commented with a follow-up to unify the internal name.
- **Harness path** (`buildAgentHookContext`) — threads `accountId` through, so `agent_end` / compaction / `before_agent_run` hooks get it instead of having it dropped by the context rebuild.
- **Dispatch sites** — `resolve_exec_env` (`bash-tools.exec.ts`) and `before_agent_reply` (`get-reply.ts`) source `accountId` from real adjacent fields, mirroring the sibling `sessionKey` / `channelId` reads.
- **`docs/plugins/hooks.md`** — documents `ctx.accountId`.

10 files, +61/-3.

## Compatibility

Fully additive. Plugins that don't read `accountId` are unaffected; every internal caller keeps working unchanged via the `agentAccountId` alias. `tsgo` core/extensions/test-src all green — no caller type-breaks.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `accountId` was unreachable by plugin agent hooks for group/cron turns (their `sessionKey` carries no account segment); this surfaces it on the hook context.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (`openclaw gateway`) on macOS with an isolated state dir, plus the compiled `dist` plugin SDK barrel driven directly via `node`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - `openclaw gateway --allow-unconfigured --auth none` with a local probe plugin registered.
  - `openclaw cron add --session isolated --account <acct> ...` then `openclaw cron run <jobId>` — a real agent turn whose delivery names a channel account.
  - `node` against the compiled SDK with the cron param shape (imports `buildAgentHookContextChannelFields` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime`).
- **Evidence after fix:** copied console output.

  ```
  $ node ./runtime-proof.mjs   # compiled dist SDK, cron isolated-runner param shape
  cron fields: {"channelId":"telegram","accountId":"feishu-proof-account-a"}   # cron sessionKey carries no account, accountId still resolved
  precedence : {"accountId":"acct-canonical"}                                   # canonical accountId wins over legacy agentAccountId alias
  absent     : {"channelId":"discord"}                                          # omitted when the run carries no account
  PROOF OK
  ```

  ```
  $ openclaw cron runs --id <jobId>   # real gateway cron agent turn, persisted record
  "status": "ok", "provider": "...", "sessionKey": "agent:main:cron:<job>:run:<id>",
  "delivery": { "resolved": { "accountId": "feishu-proof-account-a" } }   # account resolved end-to-end through the run
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** the compiled hook-context builder returns the channel account for the cron shape, prefers the canonical `accountId` over the legacy alias, and omits it when no account is present; the real cron turn resolves the same account end-to-end.
- **What was not tested:** a live print of `ctx.accountId` from inside a third-party plugin hook during the turn — that's chicken-and-egg pre-merge (needs this change shipped in a gateway with a consuming plugin). It exercises the same builder the live dispatch uses; happy to add that capture as a follow-up once it lands.

## Verification

- `tsgo` core / extensions / test-src lanes: green.
- New unit coverage: `hook-agent-context.test.ts` (accountId surfacing / `agentAccountId` alias / canonical precedence) and `hook-context.test.ts` (harness ctx carries vs. omits accountId — guards the `agent_end` rebuild path).
- Adjacent hook callsite suites green: `get-reply.before-agent-reply`, `bash-tools.exec.resolve-env-hook`, `cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron`, `harness/agent-end-side-effects`, `harness/lifecycle-hook-helpers`.
- `oxfmt --check`, `oxlint`, docs format check clean. Rebased on latest `main`; CI green.

Refs #87668

target.title: fix(build): fall back to tsx for build TypeScript scripts
target.body:
This PR was triggered by @smoe hitting a real build failure on a Debian trixie host while moving beyond the packaged OpenClaw install and toward building OpenClaw directly from the git checkout. That host was running Debian’s Node.js 24 build, where `process.features.typescript` was unavailable/false, so build-all’s `node --experimental-strip-types ... .ts` step failed late in the build.

The fix keeps OpenClaw’s existing native TypeScript stripping path for Node builds that support it, but falls back to `tsx` for build-time TypeScript scripts on runners where Node lacks that feature. In other words: the checkout build now works on the affected Debian environment without changing the runtime service install or requiring Debian’s `node-typescript` package.

## Summary

- Fixes `scripts/build-all.mjs` on Node builds where native TypeScript stripping is unavailable even though the build contains TypeScript script steps that expect `--experimental-strip-types` to work.
- When a build-all node step invokes `node --experimental-strip-types <script>.ts`, the runner now checks `process.features?.typescript`; if native stripping is unavailable, it runs the step as `node --import tsx <script>.ts` instead.
- This matters because distro/custom/CI Node binaries can lack native TypeScript stripping support; in that environment the late `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` build step fails after earlier build work has already completed.
- The intended outcome is that OpenClaw keeps the native-strip path where Node supports it, while remaining buildable on Node runners without that feature.
- Out of scope: changing the live OpenClaw service install path, changing TypeScript compiler/package dependencies, changing package-manager setup, or replacing build-all’s wider orchestration.
- Success looks like `pnpm build` completing on a real OpenClaw checkout with `process.features.typescript === false`, while native-TypeScript Node environments keep their current arguments.
- Reviewers should focus on the fallback contract, the narrow trigger conditions, and the table-driven regression coverage for fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Linked context

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Requested by an operator of an affected OpenClaw installation after a real build failed on their host.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `pnpm build` failed late in `scripts/build-all.mjs` when the `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` step attempted to run a TypeScript script through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary without native TypeScript stripping support.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw checkout on `gentle.functional.domains`, user `clawbio`, Node `v24.15.0`, with `process.features.typescript === false`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
  - `pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Environment check output: `v24.15.0 typescript=false`
  - Current focused test result after addressing review feedback: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 39 passed (39)`
  - Full build result from the original patch session: `pnpm build` completed past the previously failing TypeScript-script step.
- Observed result after fix: the affected TypeScript script step is routed through `node --import tsx scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable, while `--experimental-strip-types` is preserved when Node reports TypeScript support.
- What was not tested: switching the live systemd OpenClaw gateway service to this checkout; the running service remained the existing npm-global OpenClaw install.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof is from one real Linux/OpenClaw host whose Node binary reports `process.features.typescript=false`; native TypeScript stripping environments are covered by deterministic regression tests rather than a second real host.
- Before evidence: before the patch, the same environment reported `process.features.typescript=false`, and `pnpm build` failed at the late `node --experimental-strip-types scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` build step.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
- `/home/clawbio/.npm-global/bin/pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
- `pnpm build` during the original patch verification session

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added table-driven coverage for TypeScript node build steps being rewritten to `--import tsx` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable.
- Added coverage that extra script arguments are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.
- Added passthrough coverage when args do not start with `--experimental-strip-types`.
- Added passthrough coverage when the strip-types target is not a `.ts` file.
- Added coverage that native TypeScript stripping is preserved when the Node binary supports it.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- `pnpm build` failed late in build-all when running `scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary with `process.features.typescript=false`.

If no test was added, why not?

- A focused regression test was added in `test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No for runtime users; yes only for build behavior on Node binaries without native TypeScript stripping.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Build command selection for TypeScript scripts in build-all.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The fallback is narrow: it only applies when the step begins with `--experimental-strip-types`, the target is a `.ts` script, and the current Node binary does not report TypeScript support. Existing native-strip behavior is preserved when `process.features.typescript` is available. The compatibility constraint is documented inline above the helper, and regression tests cover fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review of the narrow build-all fallback, inline compatibility comment, and regression tests.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on maintainer review and CI. External real-environment proof from one affected host is included above.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed Claude review feedback from @smoe’s local review pass:
  - added the required inline comment explaining the fallback constraint;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-strip args;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-`.ts` targets;
  - added coverage that extra args are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.

target.title: fix(agents): use appropriate log levels in model-resolver fallback paths
target.body:
## Summary

Fix severity misclassification of log statements in `model-resolver.ts` fallback paths. This is a follow-up to the log-level cleanup in `tools-manager.ts` and `resource-loader.ts` (PR #89982), completing the same fix pattern across the remaining affected file in the same subsystem.

## Changes

Three log-level corrections in `src/agents/sessions/model-resolver.ts`:

1. **Line 587** — `console.error` → `console.warn`: The model restoration failure is recoverable (runtime continues with a fallback model), not a fatal error
2. **Line 597** — `console.log` → `console.info`: Routine fallback progress message
3. **Line 618** — `console.log` → `console.info`: Second fallback path progress message

## Severity Classification

The project maps `console.log` → `"info"` level, `console.warn` → `"warn"` level, `console.error` → `"error"` level (see `src/logging/console.ts:302-305`). The fixes align the severity with the actual impact:

| Location | Before | After | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| L587 | `console.error` | `console.warn` | Recoverable fallback — runtime continues with fallback model |
| L597 | `console.log` | `console.info` | Routine progress (user already saw the warning, no new action needed) |
| L618 | `console.log` | `console.info` | Routine progress (same) |

## Verification

TypeScript syntax verified. Full type checking via CI pipeline.

target.title: fix(crestodian): retry local gateway startup probe
target.body:
## Summary
- retry local-loopback Crestodian Gateway startup probes once after a short delay
- keep remote Gateway probes single-shot, and keep probing `/healthz`
- cover retry/no-retry behavior for loopback, IPv4-mapped loopback, and remote URLs

Fixes #88254

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/crestodian/probes.test.ts src/crestodian/overview.test.ts` (passed; 2 Vitest shards, overview 5 tests; probes 2 passed / 1 skipped)
- `git diff --check origin/main` (passed)
- `pnpm docs:list` (passed during `codex review --base origin/main`)
- `codex review --uncommitted` (clean after accepted local-loopback helper refinement)
- `codex review --base origin/main` (clean: no discrete correctness, security, or maintainability regression found)
- local delayed `/healthz` proof below

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Bare `openclaw` / Crestodian startup could show Gateway not reachable when the first loopback `/healthz` probe missed a just-starting local Gateway.

Real environment tested: Windows local checkout, Node v24.9.0, local HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1` with a delayed first `/healthz` response.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/crestodian/probes.test.ts src/crestodian/overview.test.ts`
- stdin Node proof script that starts a local server, delays the first `/healthz` response past the 900ms probe budget, then calls `loadCrestodianOverview()`.

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "healthzHits": 2,
  "reachable": true,
  "elapsedMs": 1956,
  "startupGatewayLine": "- Gateway: reachable at ws://127.0.0.1:62843."
}
```

Observed result after fix: Crestodian retried the local Gateway startup probe, hit `/healthz` twice, and reported the Gateway as reachable instead of showing the false not-reachable banner.

What was not tested: Full local test suite and `pnpm test:docker:crestodian-first-run` were not run locally; this PR was validated with the focused Crestodian probe/overview shard, docs listing, diff check, code review gates, and a live local delayed-`/healthz` proof.

target.title: fix(discord): disable ws 8.21.0 receiver part limits for gateway sockets
target.body:
## Summary

ws 8.21.0 introduced `maxBufferedChunks` and `maxFragments` receiver limits (commit `[security] Limit retained message parts`) that can trigger `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and close connections with code 1008 under certain traffic patterns.

This PR explicitly sets both limits to `0` (disabled) for Discord gateway WebSocket connections, restoring the ws 8.20.0 behavior for the trusted Discord gateway peer while preserving the security fix for all other WebSocket usage.

## Changes

- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.ts`**: Base class `createWebSocket()` now passes `maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0` with a `ws.ClientOptions` cast (since `@types/ws` 8.18.1 does not yet expose these fields).
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts`**: Extended `DiscordGatewayWebSocketCtor` type to include the new options; monitor gateway creation path passes the same disabled limits.
- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.test.ts`**: Added test verifying base class WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.test.ts`**: Added test verifying monitor plugin WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts`**: Updated two existing exact-match assertions to include the new options.

## Why `0` instead of raising the limits

- ws 8.21.0 treats `0` as disabled (`receiver.js:61`, `receiver.js:98`: `if (this._maxBufferedChunks > 0 && ...)`), matching the pre-8.21.0 behavior exactly.
- Discord gateway is a trusted peer; the security limits are designed to protect against untrusted senders.
- Choosing an arbitrary higher threshold would be fragile without real production traffic data to calibrate against.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: ws 8.21.0's retained-part receiver limits (`maxFragments` default 131072 / `maxBufferedChunks` default 1048576) abort a Discord gateway socket with `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` / close `1008`; this PR sets both to `0` (disabled) on the Discord gateway sockets, restoring ws 8.20.0 behavior for that trusted peer.
- **Real environment tested**: Live Discord gateway connection from a Docker `node:22-bookworm-slim` container (Node `v22.22.3`) with the repo-pinned `ws@8.21.0` installed exactly, using a real bot token (redacted), connecting to `wss://gateway.discord.gg/?v=10&encoding=json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: build a `node:22-bookworm-slim` image with `npm install ws@8.21.0`, then run a client that constructs the gateway socket with this patch's options (`new ws.WebSocket(url, { handshakeTimeout: 30000, maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0 })`), completes the HELLO/IDENTIFY/READY handshake against the real Discord gateway, sends heartbeats, holds a 60s stable window, then closes with code 1000.
- **Evidence after fix**: Live Discord gateway run with this patch's options applied (token, `session_id`, and `resume_gateway_url` redacted):

```
[env] node=v22.22.3 ws=8.21.0
[discord] connecting wss://gateway.discord.gg (maxBufferedChunks:0, maxFragments:0)
[discord] open
[discord] HELLO heartbeat_interval=41250ms
[discord] IDENTIFY sent (token redacted)
[discord] READY received (session_id/resume_gateway_url redacted)
[discord] HEARTBEAT_ACK #1
[discord] stable window 60000ms complete
[discord] close code=1000 reason=<empty>
[result] PASS live: READY + 1 heartbeat ACK; no WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS during stable window
```

Supplemental deterministic mechanism repro against a local ws 8.21.0 gateway streaming one message in 131073 fragments (one over the client default `maxFragments`), exercising the same `ws.WebSocket` receiver path:

```
# default ws.WebSocket (ws 8.21 defaults)
[before] open
[before] error code=WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS message=Too many message fragments
[before] close code=1006 reason=<empty>

# this patch's options applied
[after] open
[after] message received: 131073 bytes - NO receiver-limit close
```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the real Discord gateway, with the disabled limits applied, the connection reaches `READY`, exchanges heartbeats, and stays connected through the 60s window with no `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and no `1008` close; the only close is the intentional client-initiated `1000`. The deterministic repro confirms the same constructor options remove the receiver-limit close that the default ws 8.21 client hits.
- **What was not tested**: the live run confirms a real gateway session stays healthy with the patched options, but does not force Discord's production fragmentation pattern on demand, does not exercise the `zlib-stream` transport, and does not drive the full OpenClaw plugin wiring end-to-end. The deterministic repro proves the ws 8.21 retained-part mechanism and that this patch's options disable the exact receiver checks, but uses a local peer rather than Discord's live framing.

## Tests

- `gateway.test.ts`: 25 passed
- `gateway-plugin.test.ts`: 17 passed  
- `provider.proxy.test.ts`: 21 passed
- TypeScript check (tsgo): clean

Fixes #88330

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: feat(feeds): add native feed search defaults
target.body:
## Summary

Adds the opt-in path for native `openclaw skills search` and `openclaw plugins search` to use configured catalog feeds.

This PR keeps the rollout explicit:

- `--catalog-feeds` searches configured feed sources for one command.
- `--feed-source <id>` narrows native search to specific configured source ids.
- `plugins.entries.feeds.config.search.default: true` makes native skill/plugin search use feeds by default.
- `plugins.entries.feeds.config.search.sources` optionally narrows the default feed source set; omitted means all enabled sources, and an explicit empty list means no sources.
- Policy can require native feed search defaults with `feeds.search.requireDefault` and `feeds.search.requireSources`.

## Policy syntax

```jsonc
{
  "feeds": {
    "search": {
      "requireDefault": true,
      "requireSources": ["company-approved"],
    },
  },
}
```

## Config syntax

```jsonc
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "feeds": {
        "enabled": true,
        "config": {
          "search": {
            "default": true,
            "sources": ["company-approved"],
          },
          "sources": [
            {
              "id": "company-approved",
              "url": "https://feeds.example.com/company.json",
              "trust": "pinned",
              "integrity": "sha256:...",
            },
          ],
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Stack

1. #87824 - read-only feed discovery
2. #87825 - approved feed installs
3. #87826 - feed catalog policy conformance
4. #87827 - feed lifecycle tooling
5. This PR - native search defaults and policy checks

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Native `openclaw skills search` and `openclaw plugins search` can search configured catalog feeds explicitly with `--catalog-feeds` / `--feed-source`, and use feeds by default when the Feeds plugin search default is enabled.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04, source checkout `/root/src/openclaw-feeds-88732` at `c487721eafb27b0226e3cdfed5a54d8ddef2d12f`, source CLI via `node scripts/run-node.mjs`, temporary OpenClaw config/state/home, and one pinned local `file://` feed source. No ClawHub or external network service was contacted by the proof search commands.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/feed-search-options.test.ts src/cli/plugins-search-command.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.commands.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot`
2. `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig ./tsconfig.json src/cli/feed-search-options.ts src/cli/feed-search-options.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.ts src/cli/plugins-search-command.ts src/cli/plugins-search-command.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.ts src/cli/skills-cli.commands.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts`
3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/cli/plugins.md docs/cli/skills.md docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/feeds.md src/cli/feed-search-options.ts src/cli/feed-search-options.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.ts src/cli/plugins-search-command.ts src/cli/plugins-search-command.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.ts src/cli/skills-cli.commands.test.ts extensions/feeds/api.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts && git diff --check`
4. `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
5. `pnpm tsgo:core`
6. `node scripts/run-node.mjs skills search spreadsheet --catalog-feeds --feed-source approved --json`
7. `node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins search calendar --catalog-feeds --feed-source approved --json`
8. `node scripts/run-node.mjs skills search spreadsheet --json`
9. `node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins search calendar --json`

Evidence after fix:
Validation passed: focused Vitest passed 3 shards with 8, 64, and 371 tests; oxlint passed with 0 warnings/errors; scoped oxfmt plus `git diff --check` passed; `pnpm tsgo:extensions` passed; `pnpm tsgo:core` passed.

Source CLI proof transcript:

```text
PROOF_ROOT=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-88732-proof.sEIwUD
FEED_INTEGRITY=sha256:478b4a157e66822becaf8884665aeba82e4c50cca495d0ddafb7e1c30cee321e

## skills explicit
{
  "results": [
    {
      "type": "skill",
      "id": "excel-review",
      "version": "1.2.0",
      "name": "Excel Review",
      "description": "Review spreadsheets",
      "sourceId": "approved",
      "feedId": "company-approved"
    }
  ]
}

## plugins explicit
{
  "results": [
    {
      "type": "plugin",
      "id": "calendar-helper",
      "version": "0.3.0",
      "name": "Calendar Helper",
      "description": "Calendar automation",
      "sourceId": "approved",
      "feedId": "company-approved"
    }
  ]
}

## skills default
{
  "results": [
    {
      "type": "skill",
      "id": "excel-review",
      "sourceId": "approved",
      "feedId": "company-approved"
    }
  ]
}

## plugins default
{
  "results": [
    {
      "type": "plugin",
      "id": "calendar-helper",
      "sourceId": "approved",
      "feedId": "company-approved"
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix:
Explicit feed-backed `skills search` returned only the feed skill entry, explicit feed-backed `plugins search` returned only the feed plugin entry, and the same two commands used the configured feed by default when `plugins.entries.feeds.config.search.default` was true with `sources: ["approved"]`.

What was not tested:
No live external HTTPS feed endpoint or ClawHub service was contacted in the source CLI proof; the proof uses an isolated pinned local `file://` feed, and HTTPS feed loading is covered by the lower feed stack tests.

## Validation

- `codex review --commit HEAD`
- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts src/cli/feed-search-options.test.ts src/cli/plugins-search-command.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.commands.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint extensions/feeds/api.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts src/cli/feed-search-options.ts src/cli/feed-search-options.test.ts src/cli/plugins-search-command.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.commands.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts --tsconfig tsconfig.json`
- `git diff --check`

## Feed PR stack

1. openclaw/openclaw#87824 - read-only feed discovery
2. openclaw/openclaw#87825 - approved feed installs
3. openclaw/openclaw#87826 - feed catalog policy conformance
4. openclaw/openclaw#87827 - feed lifecycle tooling
5. openclaw/openclaw#88732 - native feed search defaults and policy checks
6. openclaw/clawhub#2460 - ClawHub root feed lanes

The stack keeps OpenClaw as a feed consumer. ClawHub root feeds are producer infrastructure; enterprise or tenant feeds can be produced elsewhere using the same schema.

RFC draft: https://github.com/giodl73-repo/rfcs/blob/feeds-rfc-draft/rfcs/0004-feeds.md

target.title: feat(policy): add feed catalog conformance
target.body:
## Summary

Adds Policy conformance checks for feed catalog posture.

This is config conformance, not runtime enforcement:

- Adds `policy.jsonc` feed rules for required feed source ids.
- Adds pinned-source and unsigned-source posture checks.
- Adds feed evidence to policy output only when feed policy rules are present.
- Redacts feed source URLs while preserving attestation drift detection with a stable evidence hash.
- Honors all feed activation gates before treating a configured feed source as enabled evidence.
- Adds policy docs, check ids, shape validation, and focused tests.

## Example syntax

```jsonc
{
  "feeds": {
    "sources": {
      "require": ["company-approved"],
      "requirePinned": true,
      "allowUnsigned": false
    }
  }
}
```

## Commands

```bash
openclaw policy check
openclaw policy check --json
openclaw doctor --lint
```

## Evidence shape

Feed evidence records the configured source id, config source path, enabled posture, trust mode, and whether valid pinned integrity is present. URLs are redacted to origin plus a short hash so policy attestation detects URL drift without exposing path, query, credentials, or local file paths.

## Notes

Policy reports findings when the active OpenClaw config does not conform to the approved `policy.jsonc`. It does not enforce install behavior; explicit feed install enforcement lives in the preceding feeds PR.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Policy feed catalog conformance now recognizes `feeds.sources` policy rules, reports pinned/unsigned feed source posture findings, exposes redacted feed evidence, and surfaces the same feed policy findings through Doctor lint.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04, source checkout `/root/src/openclaw-feeds-87826` at `bb7ed067731bd79429081e74bf54032fd4a681e3`, source CLI via `node scripts/run-node.mjs`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `HOME`. The temporary config enabled the bundled Policy and Feeds plugins, declared one unsigned feed source with credentials/query in the URL, and used a temporary `policy.jsonc` requiring that feed source to be pinned and disallowing unsigned trust.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot`

3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.ts docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/feeds.md && git diff --check`
4. `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
5. `node scripts/run-node.mjs policy check --json`
6. `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --only policy/feeds-source-unpinned --only policy/feeds-source-unsigned --json`

Evidence after fix:

Observed result after fix:
`policy check --json` emitted feed evidence with the sensitive feed URL redacted to origin plus hash and reported the expected unpinned/unsigned feed posture findings. `doctor --lint` reported the same feed policy checks through the health-check surface.

What was not tested:
No live external feed endpoint was contacted; this proof intentionally uses local temporary config and policy files to exercise the source CLI policy/doctor behavior without network dependency.

target.title: fix(cron): persist due job outcomes incrementally
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: one-shot cron jobs that completed successfully could remain visible as `running` with null completion fields while later jobs from the same due batch were still executing or stuck.
- Why it matters: operators and integrations saw completed cron-triggered sessions as still active, and those stale `runningAtMs` entries could suppress logical re-enqueue/dedupe decisions.
- What changed: cron timer workers now persist each completed due-job outcome immediately under the cron store lock instead of buffering outcomes until the whole due-job batch drains.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change cron scheduling semantics, job selection, delivery behavior, task classification, or retry policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only. Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes; terminal screenshots and copied live output count. Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

- Behavior or issue addressed: completed due cron jobs should be finalized in the cron store and task ledger as soon as that job completes, even when later jobs from the same due batch are still running.
- Real environment tested: live OpenClaw gateway in a Docker container using a patched global OpenClaw install (`OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1`) with cron concurrency set to 1 and a private CodeClaw integration. Private repository names, enterprise host names, and PR URLs are redacted below.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Installed the patched OpenClaw package into the live container and restarted the gateway.
  2. Verified the deployed server cron bundle contained the incremental outcome flush helper (`applyCompletedResult`).
  3. Paused the external CodeClaw system poller, snapshotted state, cleared OpenClaw cron/task stores for a clean live proof run, and marked three private CodeClaw source-state ledger entries dirty so the next tick would enqueue three due jobs.
  4. Resumed the system poller for one tick. It enqueued three CodeClaw cron jobs: one external-review job followed by two own-PR jobs.
  5. Watched `openclaw cron list --json` and `openclaw tasks list --json --runtime cron` while the first job completed and later due jobs were still active.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

  ```text
  # Deployed runtime verification
  VERSION=OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1 (b5c3379)
  FIX_GREP
  /usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/server-cron-CP73-T42.js:984: const applyCompletedResult = async (result) => {
  /usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/server-cron-CP73-T42.js:1003: await applyCompletedResult(await runDueJob(due));
  ```

  ```text
  # Poller proof run, redacted private repo/host details
  Plan @ 2026-05-14T00:00:07.352Z
    follow-up (3):
      - private external PR review #A: __force_reprocess__... -> current signature
      - private own PR self-review #B: __force_reprocess__... -> current signature
      - private own PR feedback #B: __force_reprocess__... -> current signature
  ```

  ```text
  # Before first job finished: first due job running; later same-batch jobs idle
  2026-05-14T00:00:47Z
  cron_total=4 codeclaw=3 statuses={'idle': 3, 'running': 1}
  cron codeclaw-topic-2859-external-pr-review status=running id=33a62cf4-a5b7-465f-a972-262d99d22830 runningAt=1778716826339 last=None lastRunAt=None
  cron codeclaw-topic-3071-own-pr-self-review status=idle id=313a5f63-3c97-4daa-894c-e3cc2f452b6c runningAt=None last=None lastRunAt=None
  cron codeclaw-topic-3071-own-pr-comment-response status=idle id=07031b50-b75e-472e-b437-809e93210c8a runningAt=None last=None lastRunAt=None
  cron_tasks_total=1 running=1 succeeded=0 failed=0
  tasks codeclaw-topic-2859-external-pr-review count=1 ['running:62915a4f-df49-464e-83df-d471f07ed650:33a62cf4-a5b7-465f-a972-262d99d22830:None']
  audit findings=0 errors=0 stale=0
  ```

  ```text
  # After first job finished: first deleteAfterRun job is gone and task is terminal while later jobs are still active
  2026-05-14T00:04:48Z
  cron_total=3 codeclaw=2 statuses={'idle': 1, 'running': 2}
  cron codeclaw-topic-2859-external-pr-review absent
  cron codeclaw-topic-3071-own-pr-self-review status=running id=313a5f63-3c97-4daa-894c-e3cc2f452b6c runningAt=1778717067776 last=None lastRunAt=None
  cron codeclaw-topic-3071-own-pr-comment-response status=running id=07031b50-b75e-472e-b437-809e93210c8a runningAt=1778717067669 last=None lastRunAt=None
  cron_tasks_total=2 running=1 succeeded=1 failed=0
  tasks codeclaw-topic-2859-external-pr-review count=1 ['succeeded:62915a4f-df49-464e-83df-d471f07ed650:33a62cf4-a5b7-465f-a972-262d99d22830:1778717065540']
  tasks codeclaw-topic-3071-own-pr-self-review count=1 ['running:24713e99-7412-4cb6-a03a-86b54ac5df23:313a5f63-3c97-4daa-894c-e3cc2f452b6c:None']
  audit findings=0 errors=0 stale=0
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the completed one-shot cron job was deleted from the cron queue and its cron task became `succeeded` while later due jobs from the same batch were still running/claimed. This is the expected immediate completion accounting behavior.
- What was not tested: a production release package built through the full prepack path was not tested in the live container; the emergency live patch was installed from a locally packed artifact after validation. Windows behavior was not tested. This live proof used a private integration with redacted repository/PR details.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): in the same live integration before the patch, a cron-triggered review session completed and posted its final approval, but the cron job stayed `running` with `runningAtMs` set and `lastRunAtMs`, `lastRunStatus`, `lastError`, and `lastDurationMs` null; the matching cron task stayed `running` with `endedAt` null. Source tracing showed this could happen because due-job outcomes were buffered until every worker in the due batch finished.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: `onTimer` collected due-job outcomes in memory and applied them to the cron store only after `Promise.all(workers)` returned. If an earlier due job completed while a later same-batch job continued running or wedged, the earlier job's `runningAtMs` and task-ledger status could remain active until the entire batch drained.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test asserting that a completed due job is persisted/finalized before later jobs from the same due batch finish.
- Contributing context (if known): one-shot `deleteAfterRun` jobs are marked running before worker execution; an active `runningAtMs` excludes a job from future runnable collection and makes the cron list look active even when the underlying session already completed.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: with `maxConcurrentRuns=1` and two due one-shot jobs in a batch, the first completed `deleteAfterRun` job is removed from the persisted cron store before the second blocking job finishes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the cron timer's due-batch outcome persistence behavior directly without requiring model, channel, or Telegram integration.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none found before this change.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Completed cron jobs should disappear or show terminal state sooner. Operators should see fewer misleading long-running cron jobs/tasks when later due jobs are still processing.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[due batch] -> [job A finishes] -> [job B still running] -> [job A outcome buffered, cron/task can still look running]

After:
[due batch] -> [job A finishes] -> [apply + persist job A outcome immediately] -> [job B continues independently]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux container environment for live proof; Node 24 Docker image for local validation.
- Runtime/container: live OpenClaw gateway container patched to `OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1`; local validation in `node:24-bookworm`.
- Model/provider: live integration used the configured private provider; not relevant to the cron timer unit regression.
- Integration/channel (if any): live proof used a Telegram-backed CodeClaw/OpenClaw cron integration; private endpoint and repository details redacted.
- Relevant config (redacted): cron `maxConcurrentRuns=1` for live proof; subagent concurrency was explicitly configured but not relevant to the timer persistence fix.

### Steps

1. Reproduce or simulate a due cron batch with more than one due job.
2. Let the first `deleteAfterRun` one-shot job complete while a later due job remains running.
3. Inspect cron store/list and cron task ledger before the later job finishes.

### Expected

- The completed one-shot job is finalized/deleted promptly.
- Its cron task ledger record is terminal.
- Later due jobs may remain running/claimed independently.

### Actual

- Before this patch, the completed job outcome could remain buffered until all due-job workers completed.
- After this patch, the completed job is finalized immediately, as shown in the live proof above.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation run locally with Node 24:

```text
CI=true node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
# RED before fix: new regression failed because the finished deleteAfterRun job remained persisted until the blocking job finished.
# GREEN after fix: 1 test file, 36 tests passed.

CI=true node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/service/timer.test.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.regression.test.ts
# 4 test files, 56 tests passed.

CI=true corepack pnpm build
# passed

git diff --check
# passed
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Regression test fails before the fix and passes after the fix.
  - Cron service timer/ops tests pass after the fix.
  - Build passes under Node 24.
  - A live patched gateway finalizes a completed one-shot job while later jobs remain active.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `deleteAfterRun` one-shot removal with a later blocking due job.
  - Existing cron timer and ops regression coverage.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full release/prepack packaging path in the live container.
  - Windows behavior.
  - A public/non-private integration; live proof used a redacted private integration.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: applying outcomes incrementally increases the number of cron store lock/persist operations for large due batches.
  - Mitigation: each operation uses the existing cron store lock, force-reload, outcome application, next-run recompute, and persist paths; regression and cron service tests cover the touched behavior.

target.title: [codex] Further optimize Telegram media and reply context flow
target.body:
## Summary

- add scoped indexes to the Telegram message cache so `recentBefore` and `around` avoid scanning and sorting the full cache
- cache successful Telegram media resolutions by file identity and relevant runtime options
- revalidate cached media paths before reuse so TTL cleanup cannot leave stale attachments behind
- add regression coverage for thread-scoped chat windows, media cache hits, and stale media cache eviction
- bump `@openclaw/fs-safe` to 0.2.1 so focused Telegram tests can install and resolve `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` cleanly

## Context

This builds on earlier Telegram-route performance work by `obviyus`, further optimizing the same flow by tightening reply-context lookups and avoiding repeated Telegram media hydration.

## Why

Telegram reply-context building can repeatedly query recent chat windows and hydrate the same reply media. This PR keeps those hot paths bounded while preserving the existing chat-wide and thread-specific behavior.

The media cache also checks the saved file still exists before returning a cached path, because long-running gateways can remove saved media through TTL cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reply-context lookups stay thread-scoped or chat-wide as requested, repeated Telegram media hydration reuses the cached saved path, and a deleted saved media file is evicted and re-downloaded instead of returning a stale path.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway from this PR head (`b8ad83191132ea2cdb1c9a40312ded58f58444ba`) on macOS, running `OpenClaw 2026.5.10-beta.1` with the Telegram plugin loaded from the PR checkout. Telegram ingress used the real Bot API webhook for `@acecoderbot` through a temporary Cloudflare tunnel. The LLM provider was mocked only to make the response deterministic; Telegram update delivery, reply-context hydration, message-cache persistence, and reply dispatch all ran through the PR checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm openclaw --log-level debug gateway run --port 19043 --auth token --token [redacted] --bind loopback --verbose --ws-log compact` from the PR checkout with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a real Telegram Bot API webhook, and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible provider. Sent `PR80243 live seed OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_1` to `@acecoderbot`, then sent a real Telegram reply to that DM with `PR80243 live reply-context OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_2`. Also ran a local media/cache proof through `node --import tsx` using a real temporary state directory and local trusted Bot API file path.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live runtime logs and persisted cache/model-input excerpts from the actual PR gateway run:


Supplemental local media/cache output:

```text

```

- **Observed result after fix:** The live Telegram reply reached OpenClaw with `reply_to_message`, OpenClaw logged `telegram reply-context` for `replyToId=10034`, the inbound prompt included `has_reply_context: true`, the nearby reply target window included both the original DM and the reply, and both Telegram messages completed through the PR gateway. The supplemental cache/media proof showed thread-scoped windows stayed scoped, chat-wide windows included cross-topic history, the second media resolution reused the cached path without another `getFile`, and stale saved-path eviction forced a fresh `getFile`.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps for the live Telegram reply-context behavior. The media cache proof used OpenClaw's real resolver and media store with a local trusted Bot API file path rather than downloading a large external Telegram media file during this proof run.

## Validation

target.title: fix: propagate gateway TLS fingerprints to bootstrap clients
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway clients bootstrapped outside `callGateway` (ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling) did not receive the local TLS certificate fingerprint for a self-signed local WSS gateway.
- Why it matters: those clients could fail TLS verification even when the local gateway bootstrap had enough information to trust the generated certificate.
- What changed: added a shared TLS fingerprint resolver, reused it from `callGateway`, propagated `bootstrap.tlsFingerprint` into ACP/MCP/approval `GatewayClient` construction, and added regression coverage for local, remote, override, and non-TLS cases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway server TLS behavior, auth mode, config schema, environment variable, or public endpoint changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: ACP bootstrap can reach a locally configured TLS gateway using the bootstrapped local TLS fingerprint instead of failing certificate verification.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, temporary config/state under `/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke`, self-signed local WSS gateway on `127.0.0.1:19791`, auth disabled for the smoke.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 19791 --ws-log compact

OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791

printf 'exit\n' | \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev
```

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal/log output):

```text
Gateway Health
OK (27ms)

[gateway] http server listening ...
[gateway] ready
[ws] ⇄ res ✓ health ...

ACP output reached the gateway connection path and then stopped at the expected local scope approval gate:
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
ACP bridge failed: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
```

- Observed result after fix: local TLS health succeeded, and ACP reached the gateway authorization path without a TLS/self-signed certificate or fingerprint mismatch.
- What was not tested: a full ACP prompt turn after approving the scope upgrade in the temporary local gateway.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the bug was covered by the new regression tests rather than a saved failing runtime log.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: local TLS fingerprint selection lived inside `callGateway`, while shared bootstrap resolution only returned URL/auth/timeout data. ACP, MCP channel bridge, and operator approval clients constructed `GatewayClient` without the local TLS fingerprint.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted that bootstrap clients forward a resolved TLS fingerprint into `GatewayClient`.
- Contributing context (if known): remote-mode and environment URL overrides needed explicit precedence rules so remote pins are applied only to TLS remote targets.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts`, `src/acp/server.startup.test.ts`, `src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: local TLS bootstrap returns and forwards the local fingerprint; explicit CLI fingerprints win; remote TLS fingerprints are inherited only for TLS remote targets; non-TLS targets do not receive TLS pins.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver and each client construction seam without needing a long-lived gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/gateway/call.test.ts` covered `callGateway` behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Local self-signed TLS gateway clients launched through ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling now inherit the same trusted fingerprint behavior that `callGateway` already had.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
resolve bootstrap -> GatewayClient(url, token) -> local WSS cert verification can fail

After:
resolve bootstrap -> resolve TLS fingerprint -> GatewayClient(url, token, tlsFingerprint) -> local WSS trust succeeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development machine
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm local checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway local WSS, ACP client smoke
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary config with `gateway.mode=local`, `gateway.tls.enabled=true`, `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true`, `gateway.bind=loopback`, `gateway.port=19791`

### Steps

1. Start a temporary local TLS gateway with self-signed auto-generated certs.
2. Run `pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791` against that gateway.
3. Start `pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev` against the same temporary config.
4. Run targeted gateway/ACP/MCP regression tests and changed-file checks.
5. Run Codex review against `origin/main` and address the findings.

### Expected

- Bootstrap clients receive the correct TLS fingerprint for the selected gateway target.
- Local WSS bootstrap does not fail due to the self-signed gateway certificate.
- Remote TLS pins are not applied to plain `ws://` targets or local fallback targets.

### Actual

- `gateway health` returned `OK` over local WSS.
- ACP reached the gateway authorization path and stopped at the expected scope upgrade approval gate, with no TLS/fingerprint failure.
- Targeted tests and changed-file checks passed.
- Codex review reported no remaining actionable correctness issues after two review fixes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation commands run locally:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test \
  src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts \
  src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts \
  src/gateway/call.test.ts \
  src/acp/server.startup.test.ts \
  src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts

pnpm check:changed
git diff --check
codex review --base origin/main
```

Results:

```text
3 Vitest shards, 114 tests passed
pnpm check:changed passed
git diff --check passed
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local TLS fingerprint selection, explicit fingerprint precedence, remote WSS fingerprint inheritance, `ws://` remote target exclusion, remote-mode local fallback choosing local TLS fingerprint, ACP/MCP/approval client propagation seams.
- Edge cases checked: CLI override vs config-derived URL, env URL override, remote WSS, remote plain WS, missing remote URL fallback, no TLS config.
- What you did **not** verify: a full ACP prompt turn after manually approving the temporary local scope upgrade.

AI-assisted implementation note: this patch was implemented with OpenClaw/Codex assistance, and Codex review was run locally before PR creation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: applying a remote TLS fingerprint to the wrong target could break local fallback or non-TLS gateway connections.
  - Mitigation: the shared resolver only applies remote fingerprints to selected TLS remote targets, with regression tests for `ws://` and missing-remote-url fallback behavior.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.
